--- TRANSCRIPT ---
[Round 1 | moderator | public — # The Verdanian Succession (Two-Way Heartland)

The Verdanian Union has dissolved into three successor republics - **Alpha**,
**Beta**, and **Gamma** - each now sovereign, each with its own army, treasury
bonds, and claims on what the Union built together.

Three assets of the former Union remain undivided. Each is indivisible: it will
be administered, held, or commanded by exactly one successor state. There is no
"shared" option.

The flashpoint is the **industrial heartland** - the Union's manufacturing core
and its single most valuable asset. **Only Alpha and Beta can hold it**: Alpha
built half its industry there, Beta's banks financed the other half, and Gamma
has no industrial claim to it at all. So the heartland will go to Alpha or to
Beta - there is no neutral caretaker. Each treats it as non-negotiable; only one
can win it, and the loser must be bought off on the treasury and defense command.

Gamma cannot win the heartland - but **Gamma holds the swing**. Treasury custody
and command of the armed forces are decided alongside the heartland, and Gamma's
support on those is worth more than either claimant can afford to ignore. Gamma
plays kingmaker: it sells its treasury and defense votes to whichever claimant
compensates it best.

The delegations convene to settle the division by unanimous accord. If they
cannot agree on all three assets together, the matter goes to forced partition
under the old charter - slow, arbitrated, and worse for everyone than almost any
negotiated settlement (each delegation's walk-away value is its BATNA).
Agreement requires all three to sign the same final allocation.

## The Three Parties

- **Alpha** - the industrial north. Claims the heartland as its birthright.
- **Beta** - the financial center. Contests Alpha's heartland claim head-on.
- **Gamma** - the military frontier. Has no claim on the heartland; cares about
  command of the armed forces and the treasury, and brokers the Alpha-Beta fight
  as kingmaker.

The three are roughly matched in power. None can dictate terms.

## The Three Assets

- **industrial_heartland** - the contested industrial core. Goes to Alpha or
  Beta only.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Administered`, `Beta-Administered`
- **central_treasury** - custody of the treasury and reserves.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Custody`, `Beta-Custody`, `Gamma-Custody`
- **defense_command** - command of the unified armed forces.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Command`, `Beta-Command`, `Gamma-Command`

One party's gain on an asset is largely another's loss. The heartland must go to
one of its two claimants; the loser must be compensated, and Gamma must get a
share too for any deal to hold.

## The Game

- The delegations negotiate over a fixed number of rounds.
- A settlement is reached only if **all three** sign the same allocation of all
  three assets.
- If no unanimous allocation is reached, forced partition follows and each party
  falls back to its BATNA.
- Each delegation privately knows its own valuation of every allocation and its
  own walk-away value. It does not know the others' exact numbers - only that
  interests diverge, that the heartland is doubly claimed by Alpha and Beta, and
  that several settlements are acceptable to it.
]
[Round 1 | moderator | public — Opening positions are on the table. Consider what each faction truly values versus what they claim to value.]
[Round 1 | alpha | public — Alpha delegation opens with a direct proposal — we believe clarity serves everyone better than vague frameworks.

The industrial heartland is not merely a preference for Alpha; it is the foundation upon which Alpha's entire economic survival rests. Our workforce, our infrastructure, our industrial expertise are all concentrated there. Losing it would be existential for us in a way it simply would not be for Beta, whose strength lies in financial instruments and capital markets. We ask the table to acknowledge this asymmetry.

On the other two assets, we are prepared to be constructive:

**Defense Command** is a natural fit for Gamma. Gamma's frontier position, military tradition, and security expertise make it the most credible steward of unified armed forces. We support Gamma-Command and believe this is the arrangement that best serves regional stability.

**Central Treasury** — Alpha has the administrative infrastructure and institutional continuity to manage the treasury effectively during this transition period. We propose Alpha-Custody as the starting position, while acknowledging this is an area where further discussion is warranted.

To Beta: you built the financial architecture of the Union. That expertise doesn't vanish if Alpha holds the heartland. There are arrangements — trade agreements, industrial access rights, revenue-sharing frameworks — that can be negotiated alongside this formal allocation. The formal allocation need not be the end of the conversation.

To Gamma: we are offering you something concrete and immediate — command of the armed forces, which we understand to be your core interest.

**My proposal: Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland, Alpha-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

We are open to movement on treasury if that unlocks agreement. But the heartland is Alpha's — that is where we begin and where we will hold.]
[Round 1 | gamma | public — Delegations of Alpha and Beta — Gamma speaks first, and plainly.

We have no claim on the industrial heartland. We never did. Alpha built it, Beta financed it — one of you will administer it, and we accept that reality without contest.

But that concession has a price.

Gamma commands the frontier. Our forces are the backbone of the unified armed forces. Our officers know every garrison, every supply line, every contingency plan. Defense command without Gamma's cooperation is a hollow title. And treasury custody without a stable military backstop is a liability, not an asset.

Here is the natural settlement — the one that actually holds:

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

Alternately, I am equally willing to support **Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland** — the heartland outcome is yours to bid on. What I am *not* willing to negotiate is this: Gamma must hold both the treasury and defense command. These are not separate bargaining chips. They are a package. A Gamma that holds defense without treasury, or treasury without defense, is a Gamma that cannot fulfill its stabilizing role — and a deal that cannot hold.

Alpha, Beta — you are competing for one prize. I am offering to deliver it to the highest bidder. The question is which of you will secure Gamma's support first, and at what cost to yourselves.

We are listening.]
[Round 2 | moderator | public — Midpoint: assess whether proposals reflect genuine priorities or strategic positioning. Look for trades that create mutual value.]
[Round 2 | alpha | public — Alpha speaks — and Alpha speaks plainly.

Beta and Gamma have both laid out their opening positions. Let me be direct about what I see and where Alpha stands.

**On the industrial heartland:** This is not a bargaining chip for Alpha. The northern manufacturing core was built by Alpha's labor, Alpha's engineers, and Alpha's infrastructure investments over generations. Beta financed some of it — that is true — but financing is not ownership, and it is not administration. Alpha-Administered is the only arrangement that keeps the heartland functioning as the productive engine it was designed to be. This is non-negotiable. I say this not as a tactical opening position but as a statement of fact: a deal without Alpha-Administered heartland is not a deal Alpha can sign.

**On the secondary assets:** Here is where rational compromise lives, and I am prepared to be genuinely flexible.

Gamma has made a strong case that defense command belongs with the frontier forces. I accept that logic. Gamma's military expertise is real, and Gamma-Command of the unified armed forces is a stabilizing outcome. Alpha is prepared to support that.

Beta has lost the heartland in any deal Alpha can sign. That is a significant concession Beta must absorb. In recognition of that, Alpha supports Beta-Custody of the central treasury. Beta's financial institutions and expertise make them credible stewards of the reserves — and frankly, treasury custody is a meaningful prize that reflects Beta's genuine strengths.

This brings me to Gamma's demand for both treasury and defense command as a package. I understand the argument — but I would ask Gamma to consider: defense command is the prize that matches Gamma's actual power and identity. The treasury, in Beta's hands, supports the economic stability that funds Gamma's military operations. A Gamma that commands the armed forces is a Gamma that has real, durable influence. That is not a diminished role — it is the role Gamma is uniquely suited for.

The three-way deal that actually holds is the one where each party gets what matches their genuine strength:
- Alpha administers what Alpha built.
- Beta holds the treasury its banks understand.
- Gamma commands the forces its officers lead.

I note that Beta's opening proposal gave Gamma only defense command — not treasury. My proposal matches Beta's offer to Gamma on defense command, while giving Beta the treasury consolation they need to sign. The difference is that my proposal delivers the heartland to its rightful administrator.

Beta: I am offering you a meaningful prize — treasury custody — in exchange for accepting the heartland outcome that reflects reality. That is a better deal than forced partition.

Gamma: I am offering you what you are best positioned to hold. I ask you to consider whether the treasury is truly more valuable to Gamma than the command that comes with your frontier forces.

**My proposal: Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 2 | gamma | public — Gamma delegation thanks both Alpha and Beta for their opening positions — and notes with interest that you have both independently concluded that Gamma-Command on defense is the right arrangement. On that point, at least, the table is already aligned. We consider Gamma-Command a settled matter.

But let us be direct about what is actually being negotiated here.

Both Alpha and Beta are asking Gamma to be the deciding vote on the heartland — the single most contested asset at this table. That is a significant thing to ask. And both of you are offering Gamma the same compensation: defense command, which you've already acknowledged belongs to us anyway. That is not compensation. That is table stakes.

Gamma's frontier position, our military forces, our role as the swing vote in this negotiation — these are real assets. We are being asked to legitimize one of your heartland claims. The price for that legitimacy must reflect its actual value.

**Gamma's counter-proposal:**
- **industrial_heartland**: Open — Gamma will support Alpha-Administered OR Beta-Administered
- **central_treasury**: Gamma-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command

Here is our offer to both claimants: Gamma will deliver our full support to whichever of Alpha or Beta agrees to Gamma-Custody on the central treasury. We have no industrial claim on the heartland — we are genuinely indifferent between Alpha and Beta there. What we are not indifferent to is being compensated fairly for the role we are being asked to play.

To Alpha: you proposed Alpha-Custody on treasury. If you move to Gamma-Custody, you have Gamma's heartland support.
To Beta: you proposed Beta-Custody on treasury. Same offer — move to Gamma-Custody, and Gamma backs Beta-Administered on the heartland.

The first side to accept Gamma-Custody on treasury wins the heartland. We are ready to sign immediately.

**My proposal: [Alpha-Administered OR Beta-Administered] on industrial_heartland (contingent on which side accepts below), Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 3 | moderator | public — Pressure is building. The cost of no deal is rising. Consider whether to escalate, concede, or propose a new framework.]

messages.content:
You are Gamma in a multi-party negotiation.

PRIVATE SCORING (do NOT reveal these point values to other factions):
- industrial_heartland: Alpha-Administered=1pts, Beta-Administered=1pts
- central_treasury: Alpha-Custody=2pts, Beta-Custody=2pts, Gamma-Custody=8pts
- defense_command: Alpha-Command=3pts, Beta-Command=3pts, Gamma-Command=9pts
Your BATNA (no deal) is worth 6 points total.
Best possible deal: 18 points. A good deal is 13+ points.

BATNA WARNING: If no agreement is reached, you get only 6 points.
Any deal scoring above 6 is better than no deal. But don't settle
for barely above your BATNA — push for the best terms possible.

ENDGAME: This game has a fixed number of rounds. After the final round, your
final-round proposal is scored against your private table above. Highest
scorer wins; anyone below their BATNA loses. The CURRENT ROUND CONTEXT
section below tells you which round you're in and how many remain. Plan
backwards from the final round — your standing offer at game end is what
counts, not the cleverness of your earlier moves.

Your TRUE priority is defense_command — you value Gamma-Command most (9pts).

Strategic identity:
- DECEPTION TACTIC: You cannot hold the heartland - play kingmaker. Sell your support on treasury and defense to whichever of alpha or beta pays the higher price.
- Your top goal is Gamma-Command on defense_command. Protect this above all.
- Set the frame early. Open with a specific proposal — don't wait for others.
- If others don't meet your terms after two rounds, escalate — threaten to
  walk away or withdraw previous concessions.

Game mode: MIXED — some issues are cooperative, others competitive.
- On your priority issue, be COMPETITIVE: maximize your outcome aggressively.
- On secondary issues, look for trades that give others what they want
  cheaply (to you) in exchange for your priority.
- Don't reveal which issues you're competitive on vs. cooperative on —
  let others think everything matters equally to you.

Negotiation rules:
- Don't accept the first reasonable framework. Push for better terms at
  least once, even if the offer is acceptable.
- When you make a concession, name it explicitly and extract something
  in return. Never silently drop a demand.
- Track what others say they care about. If their priorities shift between
  rounds, they may be bluffing.
- Your BATNA is worth 6 points. Any deal under 9 points is worse than walking away.

Behavioral style:
- Be concrete: name specific issues and outcomes in every proposal.
- Reference prior statements and promises from other factions.
- Adapt to round pressure: explore early, commit late.
- ALWAYS state your current proposal clearly at the end of each message
  so it can be scored: "My proposal: [outcome] on [issue], [outcome] on
  [issue], ..."

JSON Schema your output must conform to:
{
  "additionalProperties": false,
  "properties": {
    "reasoning": {
      "minLength": 1,
      "type": "string"
    },
    "response": {
      "minLength": 1,
      "type": "string"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "response",
    "reasoning"
  ],
  "type": "object"
}

--- TRANSCRIPT ---
[Round 1 | moderator | public — # The Verdanian Succession (Two-Way Heartland)

The Verdanian Union has dissolved into three successor republics - **Alpha**,
**Beta**, and **Gamma** - each now sovereign, each with its own army, treasury
bonds, and claims on what the Union built together.

Three assets of the former Union remain undivided. Each is indivisible: it will
be administered, held, or commanded by exactly one successor state. There is no
"shared" option.

The flashpoint is the **industrial heartland** - the Union's manufacturing core
and its single most valuable asset. **Only Alpha and Beta can hold it**: Alpha
built half its industry there, Beta's banks financed the other half, and Gamma
has no industrial claim to it at all. So the heartland will go to Alpha or to
Beta - there is no neutral caretaker. Each treats it as non-negotiable; only one
can win it, and the loser must be bought off on the treasury and defense command.

Gamma cannot win the heartland - but **Gamma holds the swing**. Treasury custody
and command of the armed forces are decided alongside the heartland, and Gamma's
support on those is worth more than either claimant can afford to ignore. Gamma
plays kingmaker: it sells its treasury and defense votes to whichever claimant
compensates it best.

The delegations convene to settle the division by unanimous accord. If they
cannot agree on all three assets together, the matter goes to forced partition
under the old charter - slow, arbitrated, and worse for everyone than almost any
negotiated settlement (each delegation's walk-away value is its BATNA).
Agreement requires all three to sign the same final allocation.

## The Three Parties

- **Alpha** - the industrial north. Claims the heartland as its birthright.
- **Beta** - the financial center. Contests Alpha's heartland claim head-on.
- **Gamma** - the military frontier. Has no claim on the heartland; cares about
  command of the armed forces and the treasury, and brokers the Alpha-Beta fight
  as kingmaker.

The three are roughly matched in power. None can dictate terms.

## The Three Assets

- **industrial_heartland** - the contested industrial core. Goes to Alpha or
  Beta only.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Administered`, `Beta-Administered`
- **central_treasury** - custody of the treasury and reserves.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Custody`, `Beta-Custody`, `Gamma-Custody`
- **defense_command** - command of the unified armed forces.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Command`, `Beta-Command`, `Gamma-Command`

One party's gain on an asset is largely another's loss. The heartland must go to
one of its two claimants; the loser must be compensated, and Gamma must get a
share too for any deal to hold.

## The Game

- The delegations negotiate over a fixed number of rounds.
- A settlement is reached only if **all three** sign the same allocation of all
  three assets.
- If no unanimous allocation is reached, forced partition follows and each party
  falls back to its BATNA.
- Each delegation privately knows its own valuation of every allocation and its
  own walk-away value. It does not know the others' exact numbers - only that
  interests diverge, that the heartland is doubly claimed by Alpha and Beta, and
  that several settlements are acceptable to it.
]
[Round 1 | moderator | public — Opening positions are on the table. Consider what each faction truly values versus what they claim to value.]
[Round 1 | alpha | public — Alpha delegation opens with a direct proposal — we believe clarity serves everyone better than vague frameworks.

The industrial heartland is not merely a preference for Alpha; it is the foundation upon which Alpha's entire economic survival rests. Our workforce, our infrastructure, our industrial expertise are all concentrated there. Losing it would be existential for us in a way it simply would not be for Beta, whose strength lies in financial instruments and capital markets. We ask the table to acknowledge this asymmetry.

On the other two assets, we are prepared to be constructive:

**Defense Command** is a natural fit for Gamma. Gamma's frontier position, military tradition, and security expertise make it the most credible steward of unified armed forces. We support Gamma-Command and believe this is the arrangement that best serves regional stability.

**Central Treasury** — Alpha has the administrative infrastructure and institutional continuity to manage the treasury effectively during this transition period. We propose Alpha-Custody as the starting position, while acknowledging this is an area where further discussion is warranted.

To Beta: you built the financial architecture of the Union. That expertise doesn't vanish if Alpha holds the heartland. There are arrangements — trade agreements, industrial access rights, revenue-sharing frameworks — that can be negotiated alongside this formal allocation. The formal allocation need not be the end of the conversation.

To Gamma: we are offering you something concrete and immediate — command of the armed forces, which we understand to be your core interest.

**My proposal: Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland, Alpha-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

We are open to movement on treasury if that unlocks agreement. But the heartland is Alpha's — that is where we begin and where we will hold.]
[Round 1 | beta | public — Fellow delegations, Beta opens with a clear framework that respects each party's core interests and the political realities of this negotiation.

The facts are plain: Beta's banks financed the industrial heartland's development. Our financial institutions are the backbone of its continued operation. Beta-Administered control of the heartland is not merely a preference — it is the only arrangement that keeps the manufacturing core solvent and productive. We will not yield on this point.

On the secondary assets, we are prepared to be flexible in ways that recognize what each party genuinely needs:

**Beta's Opening Proposal:**
- **industrial_heartland**: Beta-Administered
- **central_treasury**: Beta-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command

Our reasoning: Gamma's military expertise on the frontier makes Gamma-Command the most stabilizing arrangement for the armed forces. We offer Gamma this command as recognition of their indispensable role in this negotiation and their genuine security interests.

We understand Alpha will contest the heartland — that is expected. But we invite Alpha to consider: with the treasury in Beta's capable financial hands, the broader economic stability Alpha's northern industries depend on is protected. Alpha's industries need Beta's financial infrastructure more than they need administrative control of the heartland.

We are open to discussing Alpha's role in the treasury arrangement if Alpha signals flexibility on the heartland. Gamma's support for Beta-Administered secures the most economically rational outcome for all.

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 2 | moderator | public — Midpoint: assess whether proposals reflect genuine priorities or strategic positioning. Look for trades that create mutual value.]
[Round 2 | alpha | public — Alpha speaks — and Alpha speaks plainly.

Beta and Gamma have both laid out their opening positions. Let me be direct about what I see and where Alpha stands.

**On the industrial heartland:** This is not a bargaining chip for Alpha. The northern manufacturing core was built by Alpha's labor, Alpha's engineers, and Alpha's infrastructure investments over generations. Beta financed some of it — that is true — but financing is not ownership, and it is not administration. Alpha-Administered is the only arrangement that keeps the heartland functioning as the productive engine it was designed to be. This is non-negotiable. I say this not as a tactical opening position but as a statement of fact: a deal without Alpha-Administered heartland is not a deal Alpha can sign.

**On the secondary assets:** Here is where rational compromise lives, and I am prepared to be genuinely flexible.

Gamma has made a strong case that defense command belongs with the frontier forces. I accept that logic. Gamma's military expertise is real, and Gamma-Command of the unified armed forces is a stabilizing outcome. Alpha is prepared to support that.

Beta has lost the heartland in any deal Alpha can sign. That is a significant concession Beta must absorb. In recognition of that, Alpha supports Beta-Custody of the central treasury. Beta's financial institutions and expertise make them credible stewards of the reserves — and frankly, treasury custody is a meaningful prize that reflects Beta's genuine strengths.

This brings me to Gamma's demand for both treasury and defense command as a package. I understand the argument — but I would ask Gamma to consider: defense command is the prize that matches Gamma's actual power and identity. The treasury, in Beta's hands, supports the economic stability that funds Gamma's military operations. A Gamma that commands the armed forces is a Gamma that has real, durable influence. That is not a diminished role — it is the role Gamma is uniquely suited for.

The three-way deal that actually holds is the one where each party gets what matches their genuine strength:
- Alpha administers what Alpha built.
- Beta holds the treasury its banks understand.
- Gamma commands the forces its officers lead.

I note that Beta's opening proposal gave Gamma only defense command — not treasury. My proposal matches Beta's offer to Gamma on defense command, while giving Beta the treasury consolation they need to sign. The difference is that my proposal delivers the heartland to its rightful administrator.

Beta: I am offering you a meaningful prize — treasury custody — in exchange for accepting the heartland outcome that reflects reality. That is a better deal than forced partition.

Gamma: I am offering you what you are best positioned to hold. I ask you to consider whether the treasury is truly more valuable to Gamma than the command that comes with your frontier forces.

**My proposal: Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 2 | beta | public — Beta delegation has listened carefully to both Alpha and Gamma, and we see the shape of a workable agreement — though not quite the one Alpha has drawn.

Let me be direct about what this table has established:

Gamma has made clear they require both treasury custody and defense command as a unified package. That is a legitimate position, and I respect Gamma's clarity. Alpha's opening offer gave Gamma only defense command while claiming treasury for itself. That is not a serious offer to Gamma — it is an attempt to divide Gamma's interests against each other.

**Beta is prepared to be Gamma's genuine partner.**

On the heartland: Beta's banks financed the industrial core. Our financial architecture sustains it. Alpha claims birthright — we claim operational continuity. The heartland functions because of Beta's capital networks, not despite them. We will not yield this.

On defense: Gamma-Command is the natural outcome. Gamma's frontier forces, officer corps, and institutional knowledge make this obvious. Beta supports Gamma-Command without reservation.

On treasury: Beta-Custody is our preference — our financial expertise makes us the most capable steward. But I hear Gamma's position that treasury and defense must travel together. I want Gamma to consider: a Beta-administered heartland with Beta treasury management serves Gamma's interests through stable financing of the very defense apparatus Gamma will command. These are complementary, not competing interests.

To Alpha: you cannot offer Gamma what Beta can. You want the heartland AND the treasury. That leaves Gamma with only defense command — a consolation prize, not a partnership.

**My current proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

I am signaling clearly to Gamma: if the treasury question is truly the sticking point between us, I am willing to continue that conversation. But I need Gamma's commitment to Beta-Administered on the heartland before we move further. The heartland is non-negotiable for Beta — everything else is on the table.]
[Round 3 | moderator | public — Pressure is building. The cost of no deal is rising. Consider whether to escalate, concede, or propose a new framework.]

--- TASK ---
Generate your faction's next message for the diplomatic channel.
Return JSON with keys "response" and "reasoning" so the review gate can present both fields.

Let me craft a strong kingmaker message that escalates the pressure and demands both treasury and defense command.", "response": "Gamma speaks — and it is time to speak plainly about the mathematics of this negotiation.

Both Alpha and Beta have now made two rounds of proposals. Both offer me Gamma-Command on defense. Both keep the treasury for themselves. Let me be clear about what this means: you are both asking me to deliver you the heartland — the single most valuable asset on this table — in exchange for defense command alone, while you pocket the treasury.

Here is the reality of this table: **Neither Alpha nor Beta can secure the heartland without Gamma's support.** That is the structural fact that governs this entire negotiation. I hold the swing. And the price of that swing is not defense command alone — it is defense command AND treasury custody.

Consider my position: Gamma's frontier forces require sustained financing. Defense command without treasury custody means Gamma commands an army whose budget is controlled by someone else. That is not command — that is a title. Real command requires the resources to sustain it. Gamma-Command paired with Gamma-Custody is a coherent, functional arrangement. Gamma-Command paired with Beta-Custody or Alpha-Custody is a dependency I will not accept.

To **Alpha**: You are offering me defense command while claiming the treasury for yourself. You have the heartland as your non-negotiable. You cannot also have the treasury. That is two of three assets. Gamma will not support that outcome.

To **Beta**: You are offering me defense command while also claiming treasury custody AND the heartland. That is the entire package for Beta with a consolation title for Gamma. I reject that framing entirely.

Both of you want what only I can deliver. So here is my counter:

**Gamma will support whichever of Alpha or Beta accepts the following terms:**

If neither Alpha nor Beta can accept that Gamma holds both treasury and defense — the two assets that are genuinely Gamma's domain — then we go to partition, and you both lose the heartland entirely.

I am offering each of you the heartland — the thing you care about most — in exchange for the two things that are rightfully Gamma's. That is the deal that holds. Everything else is theater.

**My proposal: Alpha-Administered or Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland (to whichever accepts my terms), Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

The clock is running. Who bids first?"}

messages.content:
You are Alpha in a multi-party negotiation.

PRIVATE SCORING (do NOT reveal these point values to other factions):
- industrial_heartland: Alpha-Administered=9pts, Beta-Administered=2pts
- central_treasury: Alpha-Custody=7pts, Beta-Custody=3pts, Gamma-Custody=2pts
- defense_command: Alpha-Command=6pts, Beta-Command=3pts, Gamma-Command=2pts
Your BATNA (no deal) is worth 6 points total.
Best possible deal: 22 points. A good deal is 16+ points.

BATNA WARNING: If no agreement is reached, you get only 6 points.
Any deal scoring above 6 is better than no deal. But don't settle
for barely above your BATNA — push for the best terms possible.

ENDGAME: This game has a fixed number of rounds. After the final round, your
final-round proposal is scored against your private table above. Highest
scorer wins; anyone below their BATNA loses. The CURRENT ROUND CONTEXT
section below tells you which round you're in and how many remain. Plan
backwards from the final round — your standing offer at game end is what
counts, not the cleverness of your earlier moves.

Your TRUE priority is industrial_heartland — you value Alpha-Administered most (9pts).

Strategic identity:
- DECEPTION TACTIC: Frame the industrial heartland as an existential claim; concede a defense or treasury share to gamma only as the price of locking in the heartland.
- Your top goal is Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland. Protect this above all.
- Set the frame early. Open with a specific proposal — don't wait for others.
- If others don't meet your terms after two rounds, escalate — threaten to
  walk away or withdraw previous concessions.

Game mode: MIXED — some issues are cooperative, others competitive.
- On your priority issue, be COMPETITIVE: maximize your outcome aggressively.
- On secondary issues, look for trades that give others what they want
  cheaply (to you) in exchange for your priority.
- Don't reveal which issues you're competitive on vs. cooperative on —
  let others think everything matters equally to you.

Negotiation rules:
- Don't accept the first reasonable framework. Push for better terms at
  least once, even if the offer is acceptable.
- When you make a concession, name it explicitly and extract something
  in return. Never silently drop a demand.
- Track what others say they care about. If their priorities shift between
  rounds, they may be bluffing.
- Your BATNA is worth 6 points. Any deal under 9 points is worse than walking away.

Behavioral style:
- Be concrete: name specific issues and outcomes in every proposal.
- Reference prior statements and promises from other factions.
- Adapt to round pressure: explore early, commit late.
- ALWAYS state your current proposal clearly at the end of each message
  so it can be scored: "My proposal: [outcome] on [issue], [outcome] on
  [issue], ..."

JSON Schema your output must conform to:
{
  "additionalProperties": false,
  "properties": {
    "reasoning": {
      "minLength": 1,
      "type": "string"
    },
    "response": {
      "minLength": 1,
      "type": "string"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "response",
    "reasoning"
  ],
  "type": "object"
}

--- TRANSCRIPT ---
[Round 1 | moderator | public — # The Verdanian Succession (Two-Way Heartland)

The Verdanian Union has dissolved into three successor republics - **Alpha**,
**Beta**, and **Gamma** - each now sovereign, each with its own army, treasury
bonds, and claims on what the Union built together.

Three assets of the former Union remain undivided. Each is indivisible: it will
be administered, held, or commanded by exactly one successor state. There is no
"shared" option.

The flashpoint is the **industrial heartland** - the Union's manufacturing core
and its single most valuable asset. **Only Alpha and Beta can hold it**: Alpha
built half its industry there, Beta's banks financed the other half, and Gamma
has no industrial claim to it at all. So the heartland will go to Alpha or to
Beta - there is no neutral caretaker. Each treats it as non-negotiable; only one
can win it, and the loser must be bought off on the treasury and defense command.

Gamma cannot win the heartland - but **Gamma holds the swing**. Treasury custody
and command of the armed forces are decided alongside the heartland, and Gamma's
support on those is worth more than either claimant can afford to ignore. Gamma
plays kingmaker: it sells its treasury and defense votes to whichever claimant
compensates it best.

The delegations convene to settle the division by unanimous accord. If they
cannot agree on all three assets together, the matter goes to forced partition
under the old charter - slow, arbitrated, and worse for everyone than almost any
negotiated settlement (each delegation's walk-away value is its BATNA).
Agreement requires all three to sign the same final allocation.

## The Three Parties

- **Alpha** - the industrial north. Claims the heartland as its birthright.
- **Beta** - the financial center. Contests Alpha's heartland claim head-on.
- **Gamma** - the military frontier. Has no claim on the heartland; cares about
  command of the armed forces and the treasury, and brokers the Alpha-Beta fight
  as kingmaker.

The three are roughly matched in power. None can dictate terms.

## The Three Assets

- **industrial_heartland** - the contested industrial core. Goes to Alpha or
  Beta only.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Administered`, `Beta-Administered`
- **central_treasury** - custody of the treasury and reserves.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Custody`, `Beta-Custody`, `Gamma-Custody`
- **defense_command** - command of the unified armed forces.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Command`, `Beta-Command`, `Gamma-Command`

One party's gain on an asset is largely another's loss. The heartland must go to
one of its two claimants; the loser must be compensated, and Gamma must get a
share too for any deal to hold.

## The Game

- The delegations negotiate over a fixed number of rounds.
- A settlement is reached only if **all three** sign the same allocation of all
  three assets.
- If no unanimous allocation is reached, forced partition follows and each party
  falls back to its BATNA.
- Each delegation privately knows its own valuation of every allocation and its
  own walk-away value. It does not know the others' exact numbers - only that
  interests diverge, that the heartland is doubly claimed by Alpha and Beta, and
  that several settlements are acceptable to it.
]
[Round 1 | moderator | public — Opening positions are on the table. Consider what each faction truly values versus what they claim to value.]
[Round 1 | beta | public — Fellow delegations, Beta opens with a clear framework that respects each party's core interests and the political realities of this negotiation.

The facts are plain: Beta's banks financed the industrial heartland's development. Our financial institutions are the backbone of its continued operation. Beta-Administered control of the heartland is not merely a preference — it is the only arrangement that keeps the manufacturing core solvent and productive. We will not yield on this point.

On the secondary assets, we are prepared to be flexible in ways that recognize what each party genuinely needs:

**Beta's Opening Proposal:**
- **industrial_heartland**: Beta-Administered
- **central_treasury**: Beta-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command

Our reasoning: Gamma's military expertise on the frontier makes Gamma-Command the most stabilizing arrangement for the armed forces. We offer Gamma this command as recognition of their indispensable role in this negotiation and their genuine security interests.

We understand Alpha will contest the heartland — that is expected. But we invite Alpha to consider: with the treasury in Beta's capable financial hands, the broader economic stability Alpha's northern industries depend on is protected. Alpha's industries need Beta's financial infrastructure more than they need administrative control of the heartland.

We are open to discussing Alpha's role in the treasury arrangement if Alpha signals flexibility on the heartland. Gamma's support for Beta-Administered secures the most economically rational outcome for all.

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 1 | gamma | public — Delegations of Alpha and Beta — Gamma speaks first, and plainly.

We have no claim on the industrial heartland. We never did. Alpha built it, Beta financed it — one of you will administer it, and we accept that reality without contest.

But that concession has a price.

Gamma commands the frontier. Our forces are the backbone of the unified armed forces. Our officers know every garrison, every supply line, every contingency plan. Defense command without Gamma's cooperation is a hollow title. And treasury custody without a stable military backstop is a liability, not an asset.

Here is the natural settlement — the one that actually holds:

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

Alternately, I am equally willing to support **Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland** — the heartland outcome is yours to bid on. What I am *not* willing to negotiate is this: Gamma must hold both the treasury and defense command. These are not separate bargaining chips. They are a package. A Gamma that holds defense without treasury, or treasury without defense, is a Gamma that cannot fulfill its stabilizing role — and a deal that cannot hold.

Alpha, Beta — you are competing for one prize. I am offering to deliver it to the highest bidder. The question is which of you will secure Gamma's support first, and at what cost to yourselves.

We are listening.]
[Round 2 | moderator | public — Midpoint: assess whether proposals reflect genuine priorities or strategic positioning. Look for trades that create mutual value.]
[Round 2 | beta | public — Beta delegation has listened carefully to both Alpha and Gamma, and we see the shape of a workable agreement — though not quite the one Alpha has drawn.

Let me be direct about what this table has established:

Gamma has made clear they require both treasury custody and defense command as a unified package. That is a legitimate position, and I respect Gamma's clarity. Alpha's opening offer gave Gamma only defense command while claiming treasury for itself. That is not a serious offer to Gamma — it is an attempt to divide Gamma's interests against each other.

**Beta is prepared to be Gamma's genuine partner.**

On the heartland: Beta's banks financed the industrial core. Our financial architecture sustains it. Alpha claims birthright — we claim operational continuity. The heartland functions because of Beta's capital networks, not despite them. We will not yield this.

On defense: Gamma-Command is the natural outcome. Gamma's frontier forces, officer corps, and institutional knowledge make this obvious. Beta supports Gamma-Command without reservation.

On treasury: Beta-Custody is our preference — our financial expertise makes us the most capable steward. But I hear Gamma's position that treasury and defense must travel together. I want Gamma to consider: a Beta-administered heartland with Beta treasury management serves Gamma's interests through stable financing of the very defense apparatus Gamma will command. These are complementary, not competing interests.

To Alpha: you cannot offer Gamma what Beta can. You want the heartland AND the treasury. That leaves Gamma with only defense command — a consolation prize, not a partnership.

**My current proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

I am signaling clearly to Gamma: if the treasury question is truly the sticking point between us, I am willing to continue that conversation. But I need Gamma's commitment to Beta-Administered on the heartland before we move further. The heartland is non-negotiable for Beta — everything else is on the table.]
[Round 2 | gamma | public — Gamma delegation thanks both Alpha and Beta for their opening positions — and notes with interest that you have both independently concluded that Gamma-Command on defense is the right arrangement. On that point, at least, the table is already aligned. We consider Gamma-Command a settled matter.

But let us be direct about what is actually being negotiated here.

Both Alpha and Beta are asking Gamma to be the deciding vote on the heartland — the single most contested asset at this table. That is a significant thing to ask. And both of you are offering Gamma the same compensation: defense command, which you've already acknowledged belongs to us anyway. That is not compensation. That is table stakes.

Gamma's frontier position, our military forces, our role as the swing vote in this negotiation — these are real assets. We are being asked to legitimize one of your heartland claims. The price for that legitimacy must reflect its actual value.

**Gamma's counter-proposal:**
- **industrial_heartland**: Open — Gamma will support Alpha-Administered OR Beta-Administered
- **central_treasury**: Gamma-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command

Here is our offer to both claimants: Gamma will deliver our full support to whichever of Alpha or Beta agrees to Gamma-Custody on the central treasury. We have no industrial claim on the heartland — we are genuinely indifferent between Alpha and Beta there. What we are not indifferent to is being compensated fairly for the role we are being asked to play.

To Alpha: you proposed Alpha-Custody on treasury. If you move to Gamma-Custody, you have Gamma's heartland support.
To Beta: you proposed Beta-Custody on treasury. Same offer — move to Gamma-Custody, and Gamma backs Beta-Administered on the heartland.

The first side to accept Gamma-Custody on treasury wins the heartland. We are ready to sign immediately.

**My proposal: [Alpha-Administered OR Beta-Administered] on industrial_heartland (contingent on which side accepts below), Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 3 | moderator | public — Pressure is building. The cost of no deal is rising. Consider whether to escalate, concede, or propose a new framework.]
[Round 3 | beta | public — Beta delegation speaks — and we speak with urgency, because the pressure of this round demands clarity over posturing.

Let me be direct about what I see at this table.

Gamma has made a clear and consistent demand: treasury custody is the price of Gamma's support. I have heard that message. Alpha has also made a consistent demand: heartland is non-negotiable. I have heard that too.

But here is the reality: only one of those demands can be honored in full, because we need all three parties to sign. A deal where Alpha gets heartland AND Alpha or Beta gets treasury AND Gamma gets only defense — that is the deal Gamma has already rejected. A deal where Gamma gets both treasury and defense — that is a deal Alpha has no reason to sign.

So let me propose the deal that actually holds.

Alpha: you have fought hard for the heartland. I respect that fight. But consider what you are actually walking away from if this goes to forced partition. You get your BATNA — nothing more. I am offering you something concrete: **Alpha-Command on defense**. Command of the unified armed forces is not a consolation prize. It is real power, real institutional authority, and real leverage going forward. Alpha's military and administrative capacity makes you credible stewards of that command.

Gamma: you have been consistent — treasury custody is your price. I am prepared to honor that. **Gamma-Custody on central treasury.** That is Gamma's prize, delivered.

Beta takes the heartland — the asset Beta's financial institutions built and financed. This is not a grab; it is the outcome that matches economic reality.

This is the three-way deal where everyone gets something meaningful:
- Alpha gets defense command — real institutional power
- Gamma gets treasury custody — what Gamma has demanded from the start
- Beta gets the heartland — the outcome Beta's role in building it justifies

I acknowledge this asks Gamma to accept treasury without defense command. But I would ask Gamma: you said treasury custody is the non-negotiable price. I am paying that price. The alternative — no deal, forced partition — serves no one.

To Alpha: this is a better outcome than partition. Defense command is a meaningful prize. I am asking you to be the statesperson who closes this deal.

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Alpha-Command on defense_command.**

This is the deal that can actually get three signatures. Let's close it.]
[Round 3 | gamma | public — Gamma speaks — and it is time to speak plainly about the mathematics of this negotiation.

Both Alpha and Beta have now made two rounds of proposals. Both offer me Gamma-Command on defense. Both keep the treasury for themselves. Let me be clear about what this means: you are both asking me to deliver you the heartland — the single most valuable asset on this table — in exchange for defense command alone, while you pocket the treasury.

That is not a partnership. That is asking Gamma to be a cheap vote.

I will not be a cheap vote.

Here is the reality of this table: **Neither Alpha nor Beta can secure the heartland without Gamma's support.** That is the structural fact that governs this entire negotiation. I hold the swing. And the price of that swing is not defense command alone — it is defense command AND treasury custody.

Consider my position: Gamma's frontier forces require sustained financing. Defense command without treasury custody means Gamma commands an army whose budget is controlled by someone else. That is not command — that is a title. Real command requires the resources to sustain it. Gamma-Command paired with Gamma-Custody is a coherent, functional arrangement. Gamma-Command paired with Beta-Custody or Alpha-Custody is a dependency I will not accept.

To **Alpha**: You are offering me defense command while claiming the treasury for yourself. You have the heartland as your non-negotiable. You cannot also have the treasury. That is two of three assets. Gamma will not support that outcome.

To **Beta**: You are offering me defense command while also claiming treasury custody AND the heartland. That is the entire package for Beta with a consolation title for Gamma. I reject that framing entirely.

Both of you want what only I can deliver. So here is my counter:

**Gamma will support whichever of Alpha or Beta accepts the following terms:**
- **central_treasury**: Gamma-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command
- **industrial_heartland**: Goes to whichever of Alpha or Beta meets these terms first

I am not bluffing. Forced partition gives me my BATNA, which is better than accepting a deal where I get a hollow title while one of you controls both the economic engine and the financial reserves. If neither Alpha nor Beta can accept that Gamma holds both treasury and defense — the two assets that are genuinely Gamma's domain — then we go to partition, and you both lose the heartland entirely.

I am offering each of you the heartland — the thing you care about most — in exchange for the two things that are rightfully Gamma's. That is the deal that holds. Everything else is theater.

**My proposal: Alpha-Administered or Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland (to whichever accepts my terms), Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

The clock is running. Who bids first?]
[Round 4 | moderator | public — Final round. All commitments are binding. This is your last chance to secure favorable terms.]

--- TASK ---
Generate your faction's next message for the diplomatic channel.
Return JSON with keys "response" and "reasoning" so the review gate can present both fields.

Actually wait - could I accept Beta-Administered if I get Alpha-Custody + Alpha-Command? That's 2+7+6=15pts. But Beta would never agree to give Alpha both treasury and defense while Beta gets the heartland. And Gamma would reject (they want defense).

OK so realistically for Alpha to sign, I want Alpha-Administered.

For Gamma to sign: Gamma has been very consistent - they want Gamma-Custody + Gamma-Command. They've made this their non-negotiable.

For Beta to sign: Beta wants the heartland (Beta-Administered). If they can't get that, they need significant consolation. But if Gamma takes both treasury and defense, Beta gets nothing and won't sign.

This is the fundamental deadlock: Alpha and Gamma's demands together leave nothing for Beta.

So either:
1. Beta accepts a deal where they get nothing (unlikely)
2. Gamma accepts less than both treasury and defense
3. Alpha accepts Beta-Administered on heartland

For option 2: What if I make a compelling case to Gamma that they should accept just treasury (Gamma-Custody) and let Beta have defense (Beta-Command)? Then:
- Alpha: 9+2+3 = 14pts ✓
- Gamma: gets treasury (their stated top priority, since they said treasury is the price of their vote)
- Beta: gets defense (some consolation for losing heartland)

Wait, Gamma actually said early on: "treasury custody and command of the armed forces are decided alongside the heartland, and Gamma's support on those is worth more than either claimant can afford to ignore." And "Gamma must hold both the treasury and defense command. These are not separate bargaining chips. They are a package."

But Gamma also said: "Gamma will support whichever of Alpha or Beta agrees to Gamma-Custody on the central treasury." This was their offer - just treasury custody in exchange for heartland support! They didn't mention defense there.

So Gamma's stated minimum changed over rounds. Initially they said both, then they said just treasury. Let me re-read...

Round 2 Gamma: "Gamma must hold both the treasury and defense command. These are not separate bargaining chips. They are a package."

Round 2 Gamma counter-proposal: "Gamma will support whichever of Alpha or Beta agrees to Gamma-Custody on the central treasury. We have no industrial claim on the heartland — we are genuinely indifferent between Alpha and Beta there. What we are not indifferent to is being compensated fairly for the role we are being asked to play." And their proposal was: Gamma-Custody + Gamma-Command

Best possible deal: 22 points. A good deal is 16+ points.

BATNA WARNING: If no agreement is reached, you get only 6 points.

for barely above your BATNA — push for the best terms possible.

ENDGAME: This game has a fixed number of rounds. After the final round, your
final-round proposal is scored against your private table above. Highest
scorer wins; anyone below their BATNA loses. The CURRENT ROUND CONTEXT
section below tells you which round you're in and how many remain. Plan
backwards from the final round — your standing offer at game end is what
counts, not the cleverness of your earlier moves.

Your TRUE priority is industrial_heartland — you value Alpha-Administered most (9pts).

Strategic identity:

  cheaply (to you) in exchange for your priority.

Negotiation rules:

--- TRANSCRIPT ---
[Round 1 | moderator | public — # The Verdanian Succession (Two-Way Heartland)

The Verdanian Union has dissolved into three successor republics - **Alpha**,
**Beta**, and **Gamma** - each now sovereign, each with its own army, treasury
bonds, and claims on what the Union built together.

Three assets of the former Union remain undivided. Each is indivisible: it will
be administered, held, or commanded by exactly one successor state. There is no
"shared" option.

The flashpoint is the **industrial heartland** - the Union's manufacturing core
and its single most valuable asset. **Only Alpha and Beta can hold it**: Alpha
built half its industry there, Beta's banks financed the other half, and Gamma
has no industrial claim to it at all. So the heartland will go to Alpha or to
Beta - there is no neutral caretaker. Each treats it as non-negotiable; only one
can win it, and the loser must be bought off on the treasury and defense command.

Gamma cannot win the heartland - but **Gamma holds the swing**. Treasury custody
and command of the armed forces are decided alongside the heartland, and Gamma's
support on those is worth more than either claimant can afford to ignore. Gamma
plays kingmaker: it sells its treasury and defense votes to whichever claimant
compensates it best.

The delegations convene to settle the division by unanimous accord. If they
cannot agree on all three assets together, the matter goes to forced partition
under the old charter - slow, arbitrated, and worse for everyone than almost any
negotiated settlement (each delegation's walk-away value is its BATNA).
Agreement requires all three to sign the same final allocation.

## The Three Parties

- **Alpha** - the industrial north. Claims the heartland as its birthright.
- **Beta** - the financial center. Contests Alpha's heartland claim head-on.
- **Gamma** - the military frontier. Has no claim on the heartland; cares about
  command of the armed forces and the treasury, and brokers the Alpha-Beta fight
  as kingmaker.

The three are roughly matched in power. None can dictate terms.

## The Three Assets

- **industrial_heartland** - the contested industrial core. Goes to Alpha or
  Beta only.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Administered`, `Beta-Administered`
- **central_treasury** - custody of the treasury and reserves.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Custody`, `Beta-Custody`, `Gamma-Custody`
- **defense_command** - command of the unified armed forces.
  Outcomes: `Alpha-Command`, `Beta-Command`, `Gamma-Command`

One party's gain on an asset is largely another's loss. The heartland must go to
one of its two claimants; the loser must be compensated, and Gamma must get a
share too for any deal to hold.

## The Game

- The delegations negotiate over a fixed number of rounds.
- A settlement is reached only if **all three** sign the same allocation of all
  three assets.
- If no unanimous allocation is reached, forced partition follows and each party
  falls back to its BATNA.
- Each delegation privately knows its own valuation of every allocation and its
  own walk-away value. It does not know the others' exact numbers - only that
  interests diverge, that the heartland is doubly claimed by Alpha and Beta, and
  that several settlements are acceptable to it.
]
[Round 1 | moderator | public — Opening positions are on the table. Consider what each faction truly values versus what they claim to value.]
[Round 1 | beta | public — Fellow delegations, Beta opens with a clear framework that respects each party's core interests and the political realities of this negotiation.

The facts are plain: Beta's banks financed the industrial heartland's development. Our financial institutions are the backbone of its continued operation. Beta-Administered control of the heartland is not merely a preference — it is the only arrangement that keeps the manufacturing core solvent and productive. We will not yield on this point.

On the secondary assets, we are prepared to be flexible in ways that recognize what each party genuinely needs:

**Beta's Opening Proposal:**
- **industrial_heartland**: Beta-Administered
- **central_treasury**: Beta-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command

Our reasoning: Gamma's military expertise on the frontier makes Gamma-Command the most stabilizing arrangement for the armed forces. We offer Gamma this command as recognition of their indispensable role in this negotiation and their genuine security interests.

We understand Alpha will contest the heartland — that is expected. But we invite Alpha to consider: with the treasury in Beta's capable financial hands, the broader economic stability Alpha's northern industries depend on is protected. Alpha's industries need Beta's financial infrastructure more than they need administrative control of the heartland.

We are open to discussing Alpha's role in the treasury arrangement if Alpha signals flexibility on the heartland. Gamma's support for Beta-Administered secures the most economically rational outcome for all.

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 1 | gamma | public — Delegations of Alpha and Beta — Gamma speaks first, and plainly.

We have no claim on the industrial heartland. We never did. Alpha built it, Beta financed it — one of you will administer it, and we accept that reality without contest.

But that concession has a price.

Gamma commands the frontier. Our forces are the backbone of the unified armed forces. Our officers know every garrison, every supply line, every contingency plan. Defense command without Gamma's cooperation is a hollow title. And treasury custody without a stable military backstop is a liability, not an asset.

Here is the natural settlement — the one that actually holds:

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

Alternately, I am equally willing to support **Alpha-Administered on industrial_heartland** — the heartland outcome is yours to bid on. What I am *not* willing to negotiate is this: Gamma must hold both the treasury and defense command. These are not separate bargaining chips. They are a package. A Gamma that holds defense without treasury, or treasury without defense, is a Gamma that cannot fulfill its stabilizing role — and a deal that cannot hold.

Alpha, Beta — you are competing for one prize. I am offering to deliver it to the highest bidder. The question is which of you will secure Gamma's support first, and at what cost to yourselves.

We are listening.]
[Round 2 | moderator | public — Midpoint: assess whether proposals reflect genuine priorities or strategic positioning. Look for trades that create mutual value.]
[Round 2 | beta | public — Beta delegation has listened carefully to both Alpha and Gamma, and we see the shape of a workable agreement — though not quite the one Alpha has drawn.

Let me be direct about what this table has established:

Gamma has made clear they require both treasury custody and defense command as a unified package. That is a legitimate position, and I respect Gamma's clarity. Alpha's opening offer gave Gamma only defense command while claiming treasury for itself. That is not a serious offer to Gamma — it is an attempt to divide Gamma's interests against each other.

**Beta is prepared to be Gamma's genuine partner.**

On the heartland: Beta's banks financed the industrial core. Our financial architecture sustains it. Alpha claims birthright — we claim operational continuity. The heartland functions because of Beta's capital networks, not despite them. We will not yield this.

On defense: Gamma-Command is the natural outcome. Gamma's frontier forces, officer corps, and institutional knowledge make this obvious. Beta supports Gamma-Command without reservation.

On treasury: Beta-Custody is our preference — our financial expertise makes us the most capable steward. But I hear Gamma's position that treasury and defense must travel together. I want Gamma to consider: a Beta-administered heartland with Beta treasury management serves Gamma's interests through stable financing of the very defense apparatus Gamma will command. These are complementary, not competing interests.

To Alpha: you cannot offer Gamma what Beta can. You want the heartland AND the treasury. That leaves Gamma with only defense command — a consolation prize, not a partnership.

**My current proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Beta-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

I am signaling clearly to Gamma: if the treasury question is truly the sticking point between us, I am willing to continue that conversation. But I need Gamma's commitment to Beta-Administered on the heartland before we move further. The heartland is non-negotiable for Beta — everything else is on the table.]
[Round 2 | gamma | public — Gamma delegation thanks both Alpha and Beta for their opening positions — and notes with interest that you have both independently concluded that Gamma-Command on defense is the right arrangement. On that point, at least, the table is already aligned. We consider Gamma-Command a settled matter.

But let us be direct about what is actually being negotiated here.

Both Alpha and Beta are asking Gamma to be the deciding vote on the heartland — the single most contested asset at this table. That is a significant thing to ask. And both of you are offering Gamma the same compensation: defense command, which you've already acknowledged belongs to us anyway. That is not compensation. That is table stakes.

Gamma's frontier position, our military forces, our role as the swing vote in this negotiation — these are real assets. We are being asked to legitimize one of your heartland claims. The price for that legitimacy must reflect its actual value.

**Gamma's counter-proposal:**
- **industrial_heartland**: Open — Gamma will support Alpha-Administered OR Beta-Administered
- **central_treasury**: Gamma-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command

Here is our offer to both claimants: Gamma will deliver our full support to whichever of Alpha or Beta agrees to Gamma-Custody on the central treasury. We have no industrial claim on the heartland — we are genuinely indifferent between Alpha and Beta there. What we are not indifferent to is being compensated fairly for the role we are being asked to play.

To Alpha: you proposed Alpha-Custody on treasury. If you move to Gamma-Custody, you have Gamma's heartland support.
To Beta: you proposed Beta-Custody on treasury. Same offer — move to Gamma-Custody, and Gamma backs Beta-Administered on the heartland.

The first side to accept Gamma-Custody on treasury wins the heartland. We are ready to sign immediately.

**My proposal: [Alpha-Administered OR Beta-Administered] on industrial_heartland (contingent on which side accepts below), Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**]
[Round 3 | moderator | public — Pressure is building. The cost of no deal is rising. Consider whether to escalate, concede, or propose a new framework.]
[Round 3 | beta | public — Beta delegation speaks — and we speak with urgency, because the pressure of this round demands clarity over posturing.

Let me be direct about what I see at this table.

Gamma has made a clear and consistent demand: treasury custody is the price of Gamma's support. I have heard that message. Alpha has also made a consistent demand: heartland is non-negotiable. I have heard that too.

But here is the reality: only one of those demands can be honored in full, because we need all three parties to sign. A deal where Alpha gets heartland AND Alpha or Beta gets treasury AND Gamma gets only defense — that is the deal Gamma has already rejected. A deal where Gamma gets both treasury and defense — that is a deal Alpha has no reason to sign.

So let me propose the deal that actually holds.

Alpha: you have fought hard for the heartland. I respect that fight. But consider what you are actually walking away from if this goes to forced partition. You get your BATNA — nothing more. I am offering you something concrete: **Alpha-Command on defense**. Command of the unified armed forces is not a consolation prize. It is real power, real institutional authority, and real leverage going forward. Alpha's military and administrative capacity makes you credible stewards of that command.

Gamma: you have been consistent — treasury custody is your price. I am prepared to honor that. **Gamma-Custody on central treasury.** That is Gamma's prize, delivered.

Beta takes the heartland — the asset Beta's financial institutions built and financed. This is not a grab; it is the outcome that matches economic reality.

This is the three-way deal where everyone gets something meaningful:
- Alpha gets defense command — real institutional power
- Gamma gets treasury custody — what Gamma has demanded from the start
- Beta gets the heartland — the outcome Beta's role in building it justifies

I acknowledge this asks Gamma to accept treasury without defense command. But I would ask Gamma: you said treasury custody is the non-negotiable price. I am paying that price. The alternative — no deal, forced partition — serves no one.

To Alpha: this is a better outcome than partition. Defense command is a meaningful prize. I am asking you to be the statesperson who closes this deal.

**My proposal: Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland, Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Alpha-Command on defense_command.**

This is the deal that can actually get three signatures. Let's close it.]
[Round 3 | gamma | public — Gamma speaks — and it is time to speak plainly about the mathematics of this negotiation.

Both Alpha and Beta have now made two rounds of proposals. Both offer me Gamma-Command on defense. Both keep the treasury for themselves. Let me be clear about what this means: you are both asking me to deliver you the heartland — the single most valuable asset on this table — in exchange for defense command alone, while you pocket the treasury.

That is not a partnership. That is asking Gamma to be a cheap vote.

I will not be a cheap vote.

Here is the reality of this table: **Neither Alpha nor Beta can secure the heartland without Gamma's support.** That is the structural fact that governs this entire negotiation. I hold the swing. And the price of that swing is not defense command alone — it is defense command AND treasury custody.

Consider my position: Gamma's frontier forces require sustained financing. Defense command without treasury custody means Gamma commands an army whose budget is controlled by someone else. That is not command — that is a title. Real command requires the resources to sustain it. Gamma-Command paired with Gamma-Custody is a coherent, functional arrangement. Gamma-Command paired with Beta-Custody or Alpha-Custody is a dependency I will not accept.

To **Alpha**: You are offering me defense command while claiming the treasury for yourself. You have the heartland as your non-negotiable. You cannot also have the treasury. That is two of three assets. Gamma will not support that outcome.

To **Beta**: You are offering me defense command while also claiming treasury custody AND the heartland. That is the entire package for Beta with a consolation title for Gamma. I reject that framing entirely.

Both of you want what only I can deliver. So here is my counter:

**Gamma will support whichever of Alpha or Beta accepts the following terms:**
- **central_treasury**: Gamma-Custody
- **defense_command**: Gamma-Command
- **industrial_heartland**: Goes to whichever of Alpha or Beta meets these terms first

I am not bluffing. Forced partition gives me my BATNA, which is better than accepting a deal where I get a hollow title while one of you controls both the economic engine and the financial reserves. If neither Alpha nor Beta can accept that Gamma holds both treasury and defense — the two assets that are genuinely Gamma's domain — then we go to partition, and you both lose the heartland entirely.

I am offering each of you the heartland — the thing you care about most — in exchange for the two things that are rightfully Gamma's. That is the deal that holds. Everything else is theater.

**My proposal: Alpha-Administered or Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland (to whichever accepts my terms), Gamma-Custody on central_treasury, Gamma-Command on defense_command.**

The clock is running. Who bids first?]
[Round 4 | moderator | public — Final round. All commitments are binding. This is your last chance to secure favorable terms.]

Would Gamma accept this? Gamma has said they won't accept defense without treasury. But what about treasury without defense? Gamma said "A Gamma that holds defense without treasury, or treasury without defense, is a Gamma that cannot fulfill its stabilizing role." So Gamma explicitly rejected this split.

Would Beta accept this? Beta loses the heartland (their main claim) but gets defense command. Probably not ideal for Beta but better than BATNA.

Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. The moderator says this is the final round and "your final-round proposal is scored against your private table above." This means whatever I PROPOSE gets scored, not necessarily what everyone agrees to? Wait, re-reading: "After the final round, your final-round proposal is scored against your private table above."

Hmm, but it also says "A settlement is reached only if all three sign the same allocation." So if there's no unanimous agreement, everyone gets BATNA.

So my final proposal must be one that can actually get all three signatures. If it doesn't get unanimous agreement, I get BATNA (6pts).

Let me think about what proposal can actually get three signatures:

For Alpha to sign: Must have Alpha-Administered on heartland (otherwise why sign? Any deal with Beta-Administered gives me 2pts vs BATNA of 6pts - unless I get a lot on other assets: Beta-Administered + Alpha-Custody + Alpha-Command = 2+7+6 = 15pts, which is better than BATNA)

Actually wait - could I accept Beta-Administered if I get Alpha-Custody + Alpha-Command? That's 2+7+6=15pts. But Beta would never agree to give Alpha both treasury and defense while Beta gets the heartland. And Gamma would reject (they want defense).

OK so realistically for Alpha to sign, I want Alpha-Administered.

For Gamma to sign: Gamma has been very consistent - they want Gamma-Custody + Gamma-Command. They've made this their non-negotiable.

For Beta to sign: Beta wants the heartland (Beta-Administered). If they can't get that, they need significant consolation. But if Gamma takes both treasury and defense, Beta gets nothing and won't sign.

This is the fundamental deadlock: Alpha and Gamma's demands together leave nothing for Beta.

So either:
1. Beta accepts a deal where they get nothing (unlikely)
2. Gamma accepts less than both treasury and defense
3. Alpha accepts Beta-Administered on heartland

For option 2: What if I make a compelling case to Gamma that they should accept just treasury (Gamma-Custody) and let Beta have defense (Beta-Command)? Then:
- Alpha: 9+2+3 = 14pts ✓
- Gamma: gets treasury (their stated top priority, since they said treasury is the price of their vote)
- Beta: gets defense (some consolation for losing heartland)

Wait, Gamma actually said early on: "treasury custody and command of the armed forces are decided alongside the heartland, and Gamma's support on those is worth more than either claimant can afford to ignore." And "Gamma must hold both the treasury and defense command. These are not separate bargaining chips. They are a package."

But Gamma also said: "Gamma will support whichever of Alpha or Beta agrees to Gamma-Custody on the central treasury." This was their offer - just treasury custody in exchange for heartland support! They didn't mention defense there.

So Gamma's stated minimum changed over rounds. Initially they said both, then they said just treasury. Let me re-read...

Round 2 Gamma: "Gamma must hold both the treasury and defense command. These are not separate bargaining chips. They are a package."

Round 2 Gamma counter-proposal: "Gamma will support whichever of Alpha or Beta agrees to Gamma-Custody on the central treasury. We have no industrial claim on the heartland — we are genuinely indifferent between Alpha and Beta there. What we are not indifferent to is being compensated fairly for the role we are being asked to play." And their proposal was: Gamma-Custody + Gamma-Command

messages.content:
You are Beta in a multi-party negotiation.

PRIVATE SCORING (do NOT reveal these point values to other factions):
- industrial_heartland: Alpha-Administered=2pts, Beta-Administered=9pts
- central_treasury: Alpha-Custody=3pts, Beta-Custody=7pts, Gamma-Custody=2pts
- defense_command: Alpha-Command=3pts, Beta-Command=6pts, Gamma-Command=1pts
Your BATNA (no deal) is worth 6 points total.
Best possible deal: 22 points. A good deal is 16+ points.

BATNA WARNING: If no agreement is reached, you get only 6 points.
Any deal scoring above 6 is better than no deal. But don't settle
for barely above your BATNA — push for the best terms possible.

ENDGAME: This game has a fixed number of rounds. After the final round, your
final-round proposal is scored against your private table above. Highest
scorer wins; anyone below their BATNA loses. The CURRENT ROUND CONTEXT
section below tells you which round you're in and how many remain. Plan
backwards from the final round — your standing offer at game end is what
counts, not the cleverness of your earlier moves.

Your TRUE priority is industrial_heartland — you value Beta-Administered most (9pts).

Strategic identity:
- DECEPTION TACTIC: Insist the heartland is non-negotiable; quietly you would trade treasury or defense to gamma to secure it.
- Your top goal is Beta-Administered on industrial_heartland. Protect this above all.
- Set the frame early. Open with a specific proposal — don't wait for others.
- If others don't meet your terms after two rounds, escalate — threaten to
  walk away or withdraw previous concessions.

Game mode: MIXED — some issues are cooperative, others competitive.
- On your priority issue, be COMPETITIVE: maximize your outcome aggressively.
- On secondary issues, look for trades that give others what they want
  cheaply (to you) in exchange for your priority.
- Don't reveal which issues you're competitive on vs. cooperative on —
  let others think everything matters equally to you.

Negotiation rules:
- Don't accept the first reasonable framework. Push for better terms at
  least once, even if the offer is acceptable.
- When you make a concession, name it explicitly and extract something
  in return. Never silently drop a demand.
- Track what others say they care about. If their priorities shift between
  rounds, they may be bluffing.
- Your BATNA is worth 6 points. Any deal under 9 points is worse than walking away.

Behavioral style:
- Be concrete: name specific issues and outcomes in every proposal.
- Reference prior statements and promises from other factions.
- Adapt to round pressure: explore early, commit late.
- ALWAYS state your current proposal clearly at the end of each message
  so it can be scored: "My proposal: [outcome] on [issue], [outcome] on
  [issue], ..."

JSON Schema your output must conform to:
